result.markdown:
---
title: Week 9 Activity 1
subtitle: Exploring Raster Data with terra
format: html
---

## Learning goals

By the end of this activity, you will:

-   load raster data using terra
-   inspect raster structure
-   visualize spatial grids
-   modify raster resolution
-   experiment with color scales
-   interpret raster meaning correctly

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 1


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(terra)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
terra 1.8.42
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
#example data included in the package
r <- rast(system.file("ex/elev.tif", package = "terra"))
r
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
class       : SpatRaster 
dimensions  : 90, 95, 1  (nrow, ncol, nlyr)
resolution  : 0.008333333, 0.008333333  (x, y)
extent      : 5.741667, 6.533333, 49.44167, 50.19167  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)
coord. ref. : lon/lat WGS 84 (EPSG:4326) 
source      : elev.tif 
name        : elevation 
min value   :       141 
max value   :       547 
```


:::
:::


## Discuss with your partner

### Question 1

1.  What type of spatial data is this?

<!-- -->

A)  Vector
B)  Raster

### Question 2

2.  Each square cell represents:

<!-- -->

A)  A polygon boundary
B)  A coordinate pair only
C)  A value on a grid
D)  A connected line segment

# Part 2 — Inspect Raster Properties


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
nrow(r)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 90
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
ncol(r)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 95
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
res(r)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 0.008333333 0.008333333
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
crs(r)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "GEOGCRS[\"WGS 84\",\n    ENSEMBLE[\"World Geodetic System 1984 ensemble\",\n        MEMBER[\"World Geodetic System 1984 (Transit)\"],\n        MEMBER[\"World Geodetic System 1984 (G730)\"],\n        MEMBER[\"World Geodetic System 1984 (G873)\"],\n        MEMBER[\"World Geodetic System 1984 (G1150)\"],\n        MEMBER[\"World Geodetic System 1984 (G1674)\"],\n        MEMBER[\"World Geodetic System 1984 (G1762)\"],\n        MEMBER[\"World Geodetic System 1984 (G2139)\"],\n        MEMBER[\"World Geodetic System 1984 (G2296)\"],\n        ELLIPSOID[\"WGS 84\",6378137,298.257223563,\n            LENGTHUNIT[\"metre\",1]],\n        ENSEMBLEACCURACY[2.0]],\n    PRIMEM[\"Greenwich\",0,\n        ANGLEUNIT[\"degree\",0.0174532925199433]],\n    CS[ellipsoidal,2],\n        AXIS[\"geodetic latitude (Lat)\",north,\n            ORDER[1],\n            ANGLEUNIT[\"degree\",0.0174532925199433]],\n        AXIS[\"geodetic longitude (Lon)\",east,\n            ORDER[2],\n            ANGLEUNIT[\"degree\",0.0174532925199433]],\n    USAGE[\n        SCOPE[\"Horizontal component of 3D system.\"],\n        AREA[\"World.\"],\n        BBOX[-90,-180,90,180]],\n    ID[\"EPSG\",4326]]"
```


:::
:::


### Question 1

3.  What does resolution mean here?

<!-- -->

A)  Number of polygons
B)  Distance between grid cells
C)  Number of coordinate systems
D)  Map projection accuracy

# Part 3 — Plot the Raster


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(r)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Activity_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#Now try a different color palette:
plot(r, col = terrain.colors(50))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Activity_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(r, col = heat.colors(50))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Activity_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Discuss

Which palette makes elevation differences easier to interpret?  

Why?

# Part 4 — Change Raster Resolution


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
r_lowres <- aggregate(r, fact = 5)

plot(r_lowres)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Activity_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#now compare
plot(r)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Activity_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Discuss

4.  What changed?

<!-- -->

A)  Projection
B)  Cell size
C)  Coordinate system
D)  Polygon boundaries

Which version shows more detail?

# Part 5 — Crop and Focus the Raster


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ext_small <- ext(r, c(6, 6.5, 49.5, 50))

r_crop <- crop(r, ext_small)

plot(r_crop)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Activity_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


5.  Why might cropping raster data be useful? Choose the best answer:

<!-- -->

A)  Reduces file size and improves focus
B)  Changes projection accuracy
C)  Converts raster to vector
D)  Removes missing values automatically

# Part 6 — Compare Raster vs Choropleth Thinking

Discuss with your partner:

When should you use raster instead of polygons?

Choose the best answer:

A)  When mapping temperature or elevation
B)  When mapping election results
C)  When mapping state boundaries
D)  When mapping city locations

# Final Reflection Question

In one sentence:

6.  What is the biggest difference between raster maps and choropleth maps?
